Q1: My study routine was pretty intense but honestly kind of chaotic. I'd block out about 23 hours a week, usually in the evenings after work or on weekends when I could get to the library for their wifi. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and charts from the lecture content. Since I don't have internet at home, I had to plan everything around when I could access materials and download what I needed for later.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on when I could actually get online and what deadlines were coming up. I couldn't just log in whenever I wanted since I don't have internet at home, so I'd have these concentrated bursts of activity when I was at the library or campus. When assignments were due, I'd sometimes stay at the library for hours just to make sure I could submit everything on time. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work.
Q3: Usually it was knowing I had assignments to submit or needing to download new lecture videos and materials. I'd make a mental list during the week of everything I needed to grab when I finally got online. Sometimes I'd log in just to check announcements and make sure I wasn't missing anything important. The anxiety of potentially missing a deadline was a big motivator honestly.
Q4: Yeah, going to the library became my routine - every Tuesday and Thursday evening, plus most of Saturday. It became almost automatic, like this ritual where I'd grab my stuff, head there, and spend hours downloading, watching, and engaging with everything I could. The LMS became part of that library routine specifically. I'd have my checklist ready of what to watch, what to download, what to submit.
Q5: The lecture videos were absolutely essential for me since I'm such a visual learner. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to make detailed notes and diagrams. The discussion forums were also really helpful because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. Those 186 minutes of video watching the system shows is probably just a fraction of how much I actually engaged with that content offline.
Q6: Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip if they seemed redundant with the videos. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional interactive elements because they required constant internet connection. When you're working with limited access, you have to prioritize what's going to give you the most learning value for your time online. Some supplementary PDFs I'd download but never get around to reading.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. Visual content like videos and diagrams were always worth it for me. I also valued anything I could download and use offline later. If something required me to be constantly connected or seemed like busywork, it felt less worthwhile given my constraints.
Q8: Required activities got priority because I couldn't afford to miss them - that's why my assignment completion is so high. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, both in terms of time and internet access. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff, but I had to be strategic. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I genuinely needed extra help understanding something.
Q9: Absolutely, this was my main mode of studying honestly. I'd download everything I could - videos, PDFs, lecture slides - and then study them at home without internet. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes and visual diagrams. I'd also use textbooks from the library and free resources I could find. Most of my actual learning happened offline, away from anything the LMS could track.
Q10: Most of my study time wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd download a video once, but then watch it three or four times at home while making detailed notes. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper, creating concept maps, reviewing my handwritten notes. My 23 hours of weekly studying mostly happened offline. The LMS only saw me during my library sessions.
Q11: That was basically my entire strategy. I'd access materials once during my library time, download everything, and then study them extensively offline throughout the week. A single video download might represent hours of actual engagement at home. I got really good at planning what I needed to grab during each online session so I could be productive all week.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my stress and anxiety peaked. Sometimes I'd feel so overwhelmed that even getting to the library felt impossible. Financial stress made everything harder - worrying about bills while trying to focus on studying. There were weeks when the sadness just made it hard to care about anything, even though I knew I needed to push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed with life stuff, my LMS usage became purely functional - just submitting assignments and grabbing essential materials. The fatigue made it hard to engage in discussions or explore extra content. Sometimes I'd be so mentally drained that I'd just download things mechanically without really processing them until later when I had more energy.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be watching videos or scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely - worrying about money, feeling anxious about everything. I'd realize I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing anything. Those moments were frustrating because I was technically engaging but not really learning. The anxiety made it hard to be present.
Q15: I'd sometimes avoid discussion posts when I was feeling particularly low or anxious. Putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt overwhelming on bad days. I'd also postpone watching difficult lecture content when I wasn't in the right headspace, even knowing I was falling behind. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped sometimes, but other times I just needed to step back.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for my LMS usage. They determined when I absolutely had to get to the library, what I prioritized downloading, and how I structured my entire week. I'd work backwards from due dates to figure out my schedule. The pressure was intense but it kept me on track. My high assignment completion rate is directly tied to how seriously I took deadlines.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to keep going, especially when it was positive. Getting good marks on assignments reminded me that my strategies were working despite my limitations. Constructive criticism helped me adjust my approach for the next assignment. Sometimes waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, but seeing that my efforts paid off gave me the push to continue.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules actually worked well for my situation. I could plan my limited online time around the schedule. Having content organized into downloadable chunks helped me manage my offline studying. The structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed given how much I had to plan around my lack of internet.
Q19: The video-heavy content was great for my visual learning style and download-ability. But some interactive elements that required constant connectivity were harder for me. Discussion post deadlines sometimes conflicted with when I could actually get online. The course could have been more accessible for students without reliable internet, but I made it work through careful planning.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding, watching videos multiple times, reading through materials trying to grasp concepts before feeling ready to submit work. Or they might be dealing with anxiety about their work not being good enough. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and needs to review things repeatedly. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: That's basically my story. My attendance shows 12.8% but my assignment completion is 91.7% and I got a Merit. The LMS doesn't see my hours of offline studying, my notebooks full of diagrams, my re-watching of downloaded videos. Low visible activity can mask serious, dedicated engagement that just happens to occur outside the system's view.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students access things and what they submit. They're good at tracking deadlines and completion. But they miss so much - offline studying, the quality of engagement, emotional state, external circumstances. They can't tell if someone watched a video with full attention or while distracted. They definitely can't capture someone like me who does most learning offline.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and apply it, not just going through the motions. It's about caring enough to push through difficulties, asking questions when confused, and connecting concepts together. For me, engagement often looks like sitting at my kitchen table with downloaded materials and a notebook, completely absorbed in creating visual representations of ideas. It's internal, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS can't show my resilience - how I kept going despite high anxiety, financial stress, and limited resources. It can't show the emotional toll of managing 17 online courses with no home internet. It doesn't capture the hours I spent offline making sense of everything, or how the mindfulness intervention helped me cope even when my mood stayed low. My engagement was a daily battle that the data just can't represent.
